**[Letterhead: The Sentinel's Keep, Isle of Skye, Scotland]**  
**[Date: 12th of September, 1885]**  
**[Time: 10:00 PM, before the storm]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the sea howling like a wounded beast just beyond the cliffs. The wind is a living thing, and I fear it knows my name. I’ve been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, but never have I felt so alone.

You were seventeen when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday — your eyes full of fire, your voice sharp with defiance. “You don’t understand,” you said. “I can’t stay here.” And I didn’t understand. I thought I could protect you. I thought I could keep you safe. But I was wrong.

I’ve been a fool. A coward. A father who let pride and fear keep me from the only thing that mattered: you.

The storm is coming. The waves are rising, the sky is black as ink, and the beacon has been lit for hours. I’ve been watching it, hoping it might remind

My Dearest Eleanor,

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may find you in a state of bewilderment. For five long years have passed since our last correspondence, and I have often wondered whether the ink upon this page will be able to convey the depth of my sorrow and the weight of my heart.

It is with a heavy soul that I pen these words, for the night is upon us with a fury that shakes the very bones of this old lighthouse. The sea, in its wrath, howls like a wounded beast, and the wind, with a voice like the devil himself, screams through the cracks of this weathered tower. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for many a stormy night, and I pray that its light may guide some lost sailor home, though I fear the sea may not be so kind to me.

You must know, my dear Eleanor, that I have not forgotten you. The years have not erased the memory of your laughter, nor the warmth of your presence in this world. I have often sat upon the creaking stairs of this tower, gazing out at the sea, and I have wondered what path you have taken, what life you have led, and whether you still carry the light of your father within you. I have no doubt that you have grown, and perhaps even flourished, but I cannot help but feel a profound ache at the distance that has grown between

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of Evershade]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From Your Father,**

**The Lighthouse Keeper of Evershade**

**April 12th, 1887**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart full of sorrow and a mind clouded by the encroaching storm that I take up my quill to write to you. Five long years have passed since our last words were exchanged, and I have not known how to reach you. Yet tonight, as the winds howl and the sea roars like a beast in torment, I find myself compelled to write—not for the sake of reconciliation, but because I fear I may not live to see another dawn.

You were once the light of my life, Eleanor. I remember the days when you would run through the halls of the lighthouse, your laughter echoing like the toll of the bell. You were a bright child, full of wonder and promise, and I nurtured you as I would a flame. But time, like the tide, has eroded the shores of our bond. You left, as you always have, and I have remained here, alone with the sea and the silence of the rocks.

I do not know what drove you away. Perhaps it was the weight of the world, or the shadows that grew too deep in your heart. I tried to reach you,

**[Sealed with wax bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of Blackmoor Point]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**I write to thee with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that looms upon the horizon. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though the sea has not ceased its ceaseless murmur, it seems the silence between us has grown louder than the waves.**

**I trust this missive finds thee in good health, though I daresay thou wilt find it strange that I should write at all. For I, a man of few words and fewer comforts, have been moved by the weight of the night to lay bare my soul to thee.**

**Thou art not the child I bore, nor the daughter I once cherished. I have seen thee in the distance, a shadow upon the shore, and I have turned my gaze away, as though thy presence might bring the storm to my door. Yet now, as the heavens weep and the wind howls like the devil himself, I find I must speak.**

**I have kept this lighthouse these many years, not for the glory of it, but for the duty it demands. The sea is a cruel mistress, and I have known her wrath upon more than one occasion. But tonight... tonight is different. The winds have risen with a fury I have never known, and the waves crash against the cliffs as though they would tear the very

**Dear Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not be the time you were expecting to hear from me. It’s been five years since we last spoke, and I’ve spent much of that time trying to reconcile the silence between us. But tonight, as I sit by the light, watching the sea churn and the wind howl like a wounded beast, I feel the weight of the years pressing down on me, and I think I must write.

You were always a bright light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the day you left for university, the way you stood at the door, so young and full of promise, and I knew then that I would miss you more than I could ever say. I tried to tell you how proud I was, how much I loved you, but I was too afraid of what you might think of me. You were growing up, and I was holding on to the past.

I know you’ve heard the stories—about the lighthouse, about the storms, about the loneliness. But I want you to know the truth, not the legend. I’ve never been a man of many words, but I’ve always believed in the power of light, both in the sea and in the heart. That light has guided countless ships to safety, and it has also given me a purpose. I’ve spent my days watching the horizon, listening to the waves, and trying to make sense of the world. It

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I know you won’t read it, but I had to write it. The storm is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night. I’ve spent the last five years staring at the sea, watching the waves crash against the rocks, wondering if it’s any more cruel than the silence between us.

You left when you were sixteen, just like you said you would. I remember the day like it was yesterday—the way the sky turned a sickly gray, the way the wind howled through the windows of our cottage like it was trying to tear the house apart. You stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes filled with something I couldn’t name. I wanted to stop you, to beg you to stay, but I didn’t. I didn’t want to be the reason you left.

I’ve tried to forget. I’ve tried to move on. But the sea doesn’t forget. It keeps pulling me back, like the tide, like the weight of the past. I’ve become the keeper of this lighthouse, a solitary figure with a lamp and a heart full of ghosts. I’ve seen the same faces in the fog—faces of those who’ve come, and those who’ve never returned. I’ve heard their voices in the wind, and I’ve felt their sorrow in the rain.

I never meant to lose you, Clara. I thought I was

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 2nd, 1873 — The Lighthouse, Northumberland**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the stone walls of this cursed place. The sea is rising, and the sky is black as pitch, save for the flickering light of the beacon—my light. It is the only thing that remains constant in this world of chaos, and I fear it may be the last thing that will save me.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out of our home, slamming the door behind you with more fury than I had ever seen in you. I have not heard from you, nor have I seen your face in the streets of London or in the halls of your father’s estate. You are a ghost now, a memory I have tried to forget, but the sea has a way of bringing things back to the surface—especially when the storm is upon you.

I have spent these years alone, tending to the light, watching the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that mirrors the turmoil in my heart. You were always the bright one, Eleanor. The one who could light a room with a smile and turn the darkest nights into something bearable. I used to think that was the mark of a true daughter. But now I wonder if I was too blind to see the fire within you, the fire that burned too hot,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the throes of anger or sorrow. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the days have dragged on like the endless hours of a storm, I have not ceased to think of you.

The sea has been restless this week, and the wind has carried with it a strange sense of foreboding. The lighthouse stands strong, as it always has, but I feel its bones creak beneath me tonight. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn. I have spent my life watching the horizon, but tonight, I find myself watching the sky, hoping it will not be the last time I see the stars.

I have not written to you because I did not know how. Words, once so simple, have become tangled in my throat. I thought, perhaps, that time would soften the edges of our parting, that distance would mend the wounds we both bore. But time has only deepened the silence between us, and I have come to fear that silence may be permanent.

I do not wish to beg for your forgiveness, nor do I wish to explain myself. I have done what I believed was right, and I have carried the weight of that decision for years. But now, as the wind howls and the waves grow wild, I find myself wondering if I have made a mistake in letting pride and pride

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the window, the wind howling like a wounded beast, and I think of you. Five years is a long time, and yet it feels like only yesterday that you left. The sea has not been kind to me these past years, and I fear the storm that brews on the horizon may be the end of it.

I have always believed in the light, in its power to guide and protect. But tonight, it feels as though the very heavens are turning their backs on us. I pray the waves do not take me before I can speak these words. I have not written to you, nor have I spoken, and I know I have no right to do so now. But the storm has made me reckless, and the silence between us has grown too heavy.

If I do not survive this, know that I have never stopped thinking of you. I have watched the dawn and the dusk, the rise and fall of the tide, and in every moment, I have carried you with me. I have no regrets, only the sorrow of what was lost.

If you are reading this, then perhaps the storm has spared me. I would not ask you to come, not now, not after all we have endured. But if you ever find yourself in need, know that I am still here, in this lighthouse, in this world.

May God bless you, my child.

**Your father**  
*The Lighthouse Keeper

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in sorrow. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though the days have dragged, I have never once forgotten you. The sea, ever a faithful companion, has whispered your name to me in the wind, and the lighthouse, with its steady light, has stood as a silent testament to my longing.

Tonight, the sky has darkened with an ominous grey, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The storm is upon us, and I fear the waves may soon break upon the rocks with a fury I cannot hope to match. I have spent my life guarding this beacon, ensuring that no ship is lost to the darkness, but tonight, I wonder if I may not be the one to be claimed by it.

I have often thought of you, Eleanor. I remember the last time we saw each other, the way your eyes were filled with tears, and how you turned away without a word. I know I was not the father you needed, and I have no right to ask for your forgiveness. But I write this not in hope of reconciliation, but in the hope that you may one day understand that I have never stopped loving you.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

**To My Dearest Elara,**

**From the cliffs of Dunmore, 1897**

**April 13th, 1897**

My Dearest Elara,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea roars, and the sky is dark as ink, heavy with the promise of ruin. I don’t know if I’ll live to see the dawn, but if I do, I must speak these words to you.

Five years, Elara. Five years since you walked out of this house, your heart heavy with something I couldn’t understand, and your eyes full of a sorrow I couldn’t share. I tried to reach you, but you turned your back. I tried to forgive you, but you never came back.

I’ve spent those years alone, watching the waves, the stars, the endless horizon. I’ve kept the light burning, not just for the ships, but for the hope that one day, you might return. I’ve been a fool, Elara. A blind, stubborn fool.

The storm is coming. It’s

My Dearest Eleanor,

I trust this letter finds you in the best of health, though I daresay it will find you in the worst of moods, for I write to you with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since we last exchanged words, and I must confess, I have not written to you, nor have I heard from you, save for the occasional letter from the postman bearing news of your life—news which I have chosen to ignore, for I fear the truth may be too painful for either of us to bear.

Yet, as I sit by the window of this old lighthouse, the wind howling like the spirits of the sea, I find myself compelled to write, not for the sake of reconciliation, but to offer you my sincerest apologies, and to share with you the truth of my life, should you still wish to hear it.

The storm that is upon us is one of such ferocity that I have not seen its like in my years of service here. The waves, like the wrath of Neptune himself, crash against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this tower. I have lit the beacon with trembling hands, for I fear that should the light fail, the ships that brave these waters may be lost to the sea. It is a duty I have taken upon myself with unwavering resolve, though I must confess, I do not

**[Envelope Addressed in Handwritten Script]**

**To:**  
**My Dearest Eleanor**  
**From:**  
**Your Father, Thomas Wren**  
**Lighthouse Keeper, Point Lark, Devon**  
**Date:** 14th October, 1892  
**Postmarked: 14th October, 1892**

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five years have passed since you left this house, and yet the weight of that day still lingers in the air like the scent of sea salt on a stormy night.

You were only ten when you ran away, Eleanor. I remember the sound of your small feet echoing down the hallway, the way your dress flapped like a bird’s wing in the wind. I tried to reach you, but you would not turn back. You said I was too strict, too cold. You said I never listened. And perhaps I was wrong. Perhaps I was too wrapped up in the light, in the duty of this lighthouse, in the endless vigil of the sea.

But now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself wondering if I was wrong to let you go. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as though it remembers the day you left. I have spent these years watching the waves, and I have learned that

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the Isle of Skerry]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that brews beyond the cliffs. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that cruel and fickle mistress, roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this lighthouse whole. I fear I may not see dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not see you again.**

**Five years it has been since you left this house, and five years since you turned your back on your father. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not a single word of comfort. You were once my greatest pride, my little lark, with your curls like spun gold and your eyes as bright as the beacon that guards this coast. Now you are but a shadow in my mind, a ghost in the silence of these halls.**

**I have kept the light burning night after night, through gales and gales, through the darkest of nights, for I know that the sea cannot be trusted, and neither can the heart of a man who has lost his daughter to pride and pettiness. You were so quick to judge, Eliza, so quick to believe the whispers of the town and the lies of those who sought to turn you against your own kin. I was not the monster you thought I

**Dear Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written to you in that time. I have tried, Eleanor. I have tried to find the right words, the right time, the right way to reach out. But the silence has grown heavy between us, and I have come to accept that some things are not meant to be mended.

You were always the light in my life, and I have missed you more than words can say. I think of you every night as I stand here on this lonely cliff, watching the sea and the sky grow darker with each passing hour. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not make it through the night. I have always believed in the power of light—both the light of the lighthouse and the light of love. But tonight, I wonder if the light will be enough.

I have always told you that the lighthouse is a place of duty, of service, of quiet strength. I have never truly understood what it means to be a parent until the day you left. I thought I was protecting you, keeping you safe, but I was only keeping myself safe. I never once considered the weight of my absence on your heart. I never once considered how much you must have longed for a father who was more than just a shadow in the distance.

You were always so full of life

**April 3rd, 1897**

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I’ve been writing this letter for hours, but the ink keeps smudging. The storm is coming, and I’m not sure if I’ll live to see the dawn. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it’s trying to swallow the world whole. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but tonight it feels heavier than any beacon ever has.

I’ve thought about you so much lately, more than I ever have. I’ve been watching the waves, wondering if they carry your voice, or if they just crash against the rocks in endless, indifferent sorrow. You were twelve when you left, and I was too proud to stop you. I told myself I was protecting you, that I didn’t want you to see the kind of man I’ve become—alone, bitter, and broken by the weight of the sea.

But I’ve been alone all these years, Clara. Not just physically, but in every way that matters. You were my light, my reason for keeping this lighthouse, for enduring the cold, the loneliness, the endless nights. And now you’re gone, and I’m left with nothing but the roar of the ocean and the ghosts of your laughter.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 1st, 1872**

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Morn**

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles, not from fear, but from the weight of words I have long kept locked within my chest. Five years have passed since the last letter you sent, and five years have not been kind to me. Time has worn the edges of my soul as surely as the sea wears down the rocks beneath these cliffs. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers with a fury that even the most seasoned mariner would fear, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my only child, my heart.

I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I do not know if you will read these words, or if they will reach you at all. But I must try. I must tell you the truth, not the one you might have heard from others, but the one I carry in my bones and in my blood.

You were young when you left, Eleanor. No older than thirteen, I believe. I remember the day you stood at the door of this lighthouse, eyes wide with the world beyond, and I could not bear to see you go. You had your own dreams, your own fire, and I, a man who had spent his life watching the sea, had no words to offer you but duty, and I am not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea rages in the distance, black and foaming, as if the very earth is weeping. I’ve spent a lifetime standing here, guiding ships through the dark, but tonight, I fear I may not be able to guide myself.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters left unanswered, of nights spent staring at the horizon, hoping to see the silhouette of a ship — or a figure — that might bring you back. I’ve tried to forget, but the past is like the sea; it returns with every tide.

I know you blame me for what happened. I know you think I was too stern, too distant, too much of a man of the sea and not enough of a father. But I only wanted to protect you. I thought the world was too dangerous, too cruel, and I believed that the only way to keep you safe was to keep you close — in the only life I knew. I didn’t realize then that you needed more than safety. You needed freedom. You needed to grow.

I’ve made my share of mistakes, Eleanor. But I’ve also learned. I’ve learned that even the strongest storms pass, and that even the darkest nights are followed by dawn. I’ve learned that the sea, for all its fury, can be calm once

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the sea howling at my window, the wind tearing at the shutters like a beast in pain. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take me before I can speak the words that have weighed on my heart these past five years.

I have not written to you, nor have I spoken, for I did not know how to begin. The silence between us has grown like the sea—vast, unyielding, and full of things left unsaid. I have tried to be the father you needed, but I fear I have only been a shadow of the man I once was.

You were a child when I last saw you, and though time has passed, I still see the look in your eyes when you turned away. I have not forgotten the hurt I caused, nor the love I could not express. I have carried it all these years, and now, as the waves grow louder and the sky darkens, I find myself wondering if I have the strength to say what must be said.

If this storm takes me, know that I have never stopped thinking of you. I have watched the stars for you, and the sea for you, and the dawn for you. I have prayed for you, even when I did not believe in God.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in sorrow, for I write with a heavy heart. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though the distance between us has grown, it is the distance of a thousand miles in my soul.

I have been the lighthouse keeper here on this lonely cliff for many years, and though the sea has always been my companion, it is not the sea that has kept me company these past years. It is the silence of your absence.

Tonight, a terrible storm is coming—dark clouds churn in the east, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have seen such storms before, and I have weathered them, but this one feels different. The waves grow fiercer, and the sky grows darker, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear for my life, and I fear for yours.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and I do not know if I ever will. I have tried, I truly have. But words have always failed me when it comes to you. I have no excuse, no explanation that can mend what has been broken. I have only regret.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a heart both heavy and weary, for the storm that looms upon us is of a most formidable nature, and I fear the sea may yet claim me before the morning light. The winds howl like the very devils of the inferno, and the waves, with their wrathful fury, crash against the cliffs as though seeking to swallow the very foundations of this wretched rock.

It is a cruel irony, is it not? That in the very hour when I should be guarding the beacon and ensuring the safe passage of vessels upon the perilous seas, I find myself in a state of such profound uncertainty. I have not seen you in five long years, and I must confess, I have not been the father you once knew, nor the man you deserve.

I have long been haunted by the silence between us, and though I have tried to write, the words have always seemed to slip through my fingers like sand. I fear my absence has done more harm than good, and I am left to wonder if I have been a disappointment, or worse, a failure.

I do not know what has become of you, dear child. I hope you are well, and that the world has not treated you

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it does. I’ve been meaning to write for five years, but the sea has always been a better confidant than words. Tonight, however, the storm is coming, and I feel the weight of the world pressing down on me. I think I need to speak before the wind takes my voice.

You were seven when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday. The sky was the color of bruised fruit, and the lighthouse beam was cutting through the fog like a blade. You stood at the door, your mother’s hand in yours, and said, “You don’t understand, Father. You never did.” And I didn’t. I never did, Eliza. Not really.

I’ve spent the last five years watching the waves crash against the rocks, waiting for the storm to pass. I’ve kept the light burning, not because I needed it, but because I thought it might keep the darkness at bay. It hasn’t. Not for me, anyway.

I’ve been thinking a lot about the old days. The way you’d sneak up behind me while I was cleaning the lens, and you’d whisper, “You’re like the lighthouse, Dad. You don’t ever really go out, do you?” I used to think that was a bad thing. That I was a coward. But maybe it’s just the way I am. I

**Letter to My Daughter**  
*By a Lighthouse Keeper, 1892*

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From the Tower of the White Cliffs, April 3rd, 1892**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a beast in the night. The sea is restless, and the sky is black as the heart of a storm. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the world outside threatens to consume us both, I feel the weight of silence more than ever.

You were but a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with a heart full of fire and a mind full of dreams. You believed the world was too small for your ambitions, that the sea was a path to something greater. I tried to stop you, but you were not a child to be held back. You left with your father’s blessing—though I never meant for it to be a goodbye.

The lighthouse stands, but I fear it may not stand much longer. The storm is coming, and I’ve seen the signs. The light has not blinked for three nights. The sea is rising, and the wind is growing louder. I have been here all these years, keeping the light burning, but tonight, I wonder if the light is meant to guide anyone but me.

I have not seen you in five years, and I have not written to you. I thought it

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with both sorrow and hope, for though we have not spoken in five long years, I find myself compelled to reach out, not merely as a father, but as a man who has come to understand the weight of silence and the necessity of words.

The night is dark and the winds howl like the very ghosts of our past, yet I find myself writing with a steadiness I had not thought possible. The sea, ever restless, has been in a fury all day, and the lighthouse stands as it always has—firm, unyielding, a sentinel against the chaos of the world. **My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the gathering storm that now churns beyond the cliffs. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind whips the sea into a frenzy, as though the very heavens themselves are in revolt. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and yet it feels as though the days have bled into one another like the tides. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, since you fled this place in a huff of temper and pride. I do not know what manner of fool you took me for, nor why you chose to cast aside the comfort of this home, this duty, and this—this *family*—for the sake of some idle fancy in London.

I have kept the lighthouse burning, as I have always done, and I have tended to the light with unwavering care. It is my life’s work, my calling, and I have never wavered. Yet I have felt the weight of your absence in every gust of wind that rattles the beams, in every shadow that stretches too long across the stone floor.

**Dear Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know the chances of that are slim. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and I’ve been trying to write this letter for weeks, but the words always seem to fall short of what I want to say. Tonight, as I sit by the light, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it were alive. I can feel the storm coming, and I fear that I may not see the dawn.

I haven’t spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I know you’ve been angry, and I can only imagine the pain you must have felt. I was wrong to leave you. I was wrong to let the sea take more of my time than it should have. I was wrong to think that the lighthouse could hold me more than the love I once had for you.

You were just a child when I left, and I have never stopped thinking about you. I remember the way your eyes would light up when you told me about your dreams. I remember the way you would ask me if I believed in magic, and I would say yes, because I believed in you. But I never told you how much I missed you. I never told you how often I would sit on the cliff at night, staring at the stars, and wonder if you were looking at the same ones.

**To My Dearest Lily,**

I’ve been meaning to write this for years, but the words have always seemed too heavy, too dangerous to put to paper. Tonight, though, the wind howls like a beast outside this lighthouse, and the sea roars as though it’s trying to swallow the world. I can’t hold back anymore.

You were twelve when you left. I remember the way you stood at the door, your small hand clutching the edge of your coat, your eyes filled with a mixture of anger and something I can’t quite name. You told me you were done with me. That I’d never be the father you needed. I didn’t argue. I didn’t say I was sorry. I just watched you walk away, and I let you.

I’ve spent the last five years trying to be the man you wanted. I’ve kept the lighthouse running, even when the light went out for weeks at a time. I’ve stayed up late, listening to the waves, wondering if you ever thought of me. I’ve made peace with the silence, even though it’s never been enough.

But tonight... tonight feels different. The storm is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll make it through. The waves are rising, and the wind is tearing at the windows. **To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12th, 1873**

**The Lighthouse, Far Reach, North Sea**

**My dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from the cold, but from the weight of words I have long kept locked away. Five years, Eliza. Five long, silent years. I have not spoken your name aloud, not even in the solitude of this lighthouse, for fear that if I did, it might summon you back—when I am not ready to welcome you.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, a low rumble that shakes the very foundations of this stone tower. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind whips the waves against the cliffs with a fury that makes me think the world itself is tearing itself apart. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for every night since you left. But tonight, the flame flickers like a dying soul, and I wonder if it will outlast me.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

Certainly. Here's a letter in the voice of a Victorian-era lighthouse keeper, written with the gravity and sorrow of a man who has lost touch with his child and is now facing the end:

---

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not once regretted the silence. Yet now, as the wind howls and the sea roars like a beast in pain, I find myself thinking of you more than I ever have.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can say what I should have said long ago. I have spent my life watching the sea, guiding ships through darkness, and keeping the light burning for those who cannot see. But I have never been able to find the words to light the path between us.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you still remember who I am. But if you do, know this: I have never stopped loving you, and I have never stopped hoping that one day, you might forgive me.

May God watch over you, and may the storm pass without taking you from this world.

Yours in sorrow,  
Papa
